title: Privacy-Preserving Prototype-Based Federated Learning for Lung Cancer Forecasting
authors: Qingchen Zhang, Hang Yu, Yu Guo, Chenhui Yao, Chundong Gu
abstract: Lung cancer remains a leading cause of death worldwide, and reliable subtype identification from computed tomography (CT) is hindered by limited per-subtype data, privacy regulations, and distribution shifts across hospitals. We propose a prototype-based personalized federated learning framework that classifies lung-cancer subtypes directly from lesion-centric CT volumes while preserving privacy. A 3D SEResNet backbone provides embeddings for a prototypical network; prototype similarity between institutions is computed to select the most compatible source model for a requesting site. To protect sensitive information, similarity is computed under Cheon–Kim–Kim–Song (CKKS) homomorphic encryption, and training employs differentially private SGD, yielding quantified privacy without sharing raw images or intermediate features. Using multi-institution CT cohorts from The Cancer Imaging Archive (TCIA), we conduct few-shot, 4-way meta-testing that reflects realistic inter-site heterogeneity. The proposed approach consistently improves over global aggregation baselines, remains robust under practical privacy budgets, and is particularly effective when moderate shots per class are available. These results on the TCIA collections demonstrate a viable path to secure, personalized, and data-efficient lung-cancer subtype prediction, enabling multi-center collaboration without compromising patient privacy.

title: A Non-Ecap Wide Range Bidirectional OBC With Multi-function Circuit
authors: Hai N. Tran, Long H. B. Nguyen, Minh V. Vo, Tuyen D. Nguyen
abstract: The rapid growth of Electric Vehicles (EVs) with larger battery capacities and integrated Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X) applications is accelerating the demand for high-power Bidirectional On-Board Chargers (BOBCs). Conventional BOBC architectures, typically based on Frequency Controlled (FC) CLLLC converters with large Electrolytic Capacitor (E-cap or Ecap), face three critical limitations: 1) low power density and short lifetime of E-cap; 2) complex resonant tank design requiring dual resonant networks for bidirectionality in addition to low inductance ratio for voltage regulation purposes, and 3) high MOSFET turn-off current at the CLLLC stage that elevates switching losses, voltage overshoot, and reliability risks. While active Power Decoupling (PD) concepts have been proposed to mitigate E-cap issues, these solutions often require high capacitance, are only partially utilized in 3-phase mode, and still face reliability challenges in the isolated stage. This paper introduces a novel BOBC architecture featuring a Multi-function Circuit (MC) placed between the AFE and isolated stage. The MC simultaneously performs power decoupling and charging current regulation, enabling the isolated converter to operate purely as a DC transformer (DCX) for galvanic isolation. This MC-DCX approach eliminates bulky E-cap, reduces semiconductor stress, simplifies resonant tank design, improves system reliability, and enhances scalability to both 400V and 800V platforms. A detailed comparison at 22 kW shows lower switching losses, reduced resonant capacitor stress, and improved reliability versus conventional CLLLC designs. The proposed BOBC, integrated with an Auxiliary Power Module (APM) into a compact Integrated Dual Charging Module (IDCM), supports universal grid with 11 kW 1-phase and 22 kW 3-phase charging. Experimental validation confirms the feasibility of the concept. This paper is among the first to provide a systematic comparison of MC-DCX with FC-LLC at the 22 kW level, offering valuable guidance for academic and industrial as BOBCs transition toward higher voltage, higher power, and wider operating ranges.

title: World Model for Autonomous Information Freshness in Sparse-Reward Mobile Edge Networks
authors: Xiaozhe Wang, Renpeng Liu, Bo Hu, Shanzhi Chen
abstract: Generative world models provide a promising computational paradigm for autonomous decision-making in sparse-reward environments. However, applying these models to Age of Information (AoI) minimization in mobile edge networks faces a fundamental temporal structural mismatch, characterized by the conflict between continuous energy expenditure and discrete, event-triggered information updates. This letter proposes aWorld Model-driven Generative Active Inference (WM-GAI) method to address this issue. WM-GAI constructs a latent state-space dynamics model to anticipate future channel variations and information gain evolution via latent rollouts, avoiding costly physical trial-and-error. Furthermore, we introduce a contrastive prior mechanism to anchor the acquisition policy to historical high-utility states, guiding the agent toward informative regions in sparse-reward environments. Simulation results demonstrate that WM-GAI reduces the system average AoI by 12% and improves sample efficiency by 25% compared to the baseline.

title: Window-Free Transformer for Efficient Single Image Super-Resolution
authors: Xue Wu, Kaibing Zhang, Jingwei Xin, Nannan Wang, Xinbo Gao
abstract: Vision Transformer-based models have shown promising performance on various image recovery tasks including image super-resolution (SR). However, their intensive computational complexity hinders the applicability, particularly in resource-limited scenarios. To alleviate this weakness, developing a lightweight yet effective transformer model is worthy of further investigation. In this paper, we elaborate on a novel Window-Free Transformer (WFT) for efficient SR. The WFT consists of three core components, namely multi-head multi-scale spatial self-attention (MMSSA), multi-head channel self-attention (MCSA), and dual spatial-gate feed-forward network (DSGFN). Specifically, the MMSSA achieves long-range dependencies by splitting the attention head into different groups and each group performs spatial self-attention across different scales. The MCSA performs global channel self-attention by measuring attention scores between different channel tokens to strengthen feature interactions across all channels. MMSSA and MCSA can conquer the bottleneck of window-constraint schemes and yield sufficient receptive fields. Additionally, the DSGFN is designed to enhance local contextual information via a dual spatial-gate mechanism. Thorough experimental evaluations demonstrate that the WFT outperforms the existing leading SR methods on five benchmark datasets in both quantitative and qualitative assessments while maintaining a compact model size. Specifically, WFT outperforms SwinIR-light and SRFormer-light by up to 0.38dB and 0.33dB in PSNR on the Urban100 dataset, and by up to 0.41dB and 0.39dB on the Manga109 dataset, respectively, while requiring fewer parameters and computations. Moreover, our lightweight WFT can be scaled to the classical SR task, delivering remarkable SR performance over other competitors. To facilitate reproducibility, we will release the source code upon publication.

title: Photon-Counting Lidar Remote Sensing: Current progress and future trends
authors: Lin Wu, Yifu Chen, Yuan Le, Dongfang Zhang, Xiaohan Zhang, Jun Li, Lizhe Wang
publish: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Magazine
abstract: This article provides a comprehensive review of photon-counting lidar systems. With the rapid advancement of photon-counting detectors and their supporting technologies, these systems—characterized by their sensitivity to individual photons—have emerged as a promising solution for next-generation active remote sensing. Photon-counting lidar systems facilitate high-resolution, large-scale 3D Earth observation with extremely low energy consumption, making them a crucial technical foundation for future remote sensing applications.

title: UDP: Up-or-Down Precision Quantization for Large Language Models via Evolutionary Search
authors: Anh-Tuan Mai, Tien-Son Pham, Xuan Truong Nguyen, Thanh-Tuan Dao
abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have shown outstanding capabilities across complex language tasks, but come with massive computational cost and memory footprint. LLM post-training quantizers such as AWQ, SmoothQuant, and QoQ have demonstrated excellent performance under specific configurations, for example, W4A16 (four-bit weight and sixteen-bit activation) and W8A8 (eight-bit weight and eight-bit activation). However, leveraging these quantizers in a mixed-precision problem is a nontrivial task due to the colossal design space and prolonged inference time in LLMs. To address this problem, this work presents UDP (Up-or-Down Precision), a quantizer-agnostic mixed-precision framework that restricts each layer to a low/high precision pair and selects which layers to promote under deployment-driven memory/latency budgets. UDP uses KLD-guided profiling to seed high-quality candidates and a budget-aware genetic search to optimize the layer promotion set. Across Llama and Mistral models and three PTQ backends (AWQ, SmoothQuant, QoQ), UDP improvesWikiText-2 perplexity by up to 0.69 over uniform low-bit baselines at the same low-precision setting and outperforms strong mixed-precision heuristics (FlattenQuant, QFeM, AsymKV) by up to 0.11 under identical budgets. In OmniServe on Llama-2-7B (1024/512 tokens), UDP preserves throughput (≤0.5% vs. W4A8) while reducing p95 time-to-first-token by ≈60 ms and using ≈10% less GPU memory than W8A8. These results show UDP produces practical mixed-precision policies aligned with real serving constraints.

title: Camera noise extraction from a raw single image by segmentation, multiple approximations, and weighted averaging
authors: Alexander V. Kozlov, Pavel A. Cheremkhin, Anton A. Volkov, Andrey S. Svistunov, Rostislav S. Starikov, Vsevolod A. Nebavskiy, Evgenii Yu. Zlokazov, Vladislav G. Rodin
abstract: Digital cameras are widely used in a variety of applications, including medicine, security, material characterization, and metrology. To reduce cost, increase dynamic range, and minimize noise, accurate knowledge of camera parameters, including noise characteristics, is required. The most promising camera noise estimation methods use a single image. However, most methods estimate the noise of the acquired image rather than the intrinsic sensor noise of the camera. In this paper, a method is proposed to estimate main noise components from a single image: light temporal, light spatial and dark noise The method is based on segmentation, multi-interval approximation, and weighted averaging. The method was experimentally validated on a dataset obtained from a camera whose noise parameters were independently measured. The noise estimation error was reduced to single-digit or several tens of percent, which is more than 5 times better than using patch-based approximation. The proposed method can be used in signal and image processing, for camera selection, noise reduction, image authenticity, and so on.

title: Incentive-Based Demand Response via Smart Predict-then-Optimize with Neural Networks
authors: Makoto Tanaka
abstract: The conventional predict-then-optimize (PO) approach minimizes prediction error in sequential predictive and optimization processes. However, high prediction accuracy does not guarantee high-quality decision-making. This paper applies the smart predict-then-optimize (SPO) framework to incentive-based demand response programs, where predictive models are trained to minimize decision regret rather than prediction error. We develop an integrated approach combining neural network-based baseline load prediction with the system operator’s optimization objective for load reduction procurement. Additionally, we consider Wasserstein distributionally robust optimization (DRO) as an alternative method of handling uncertainty in the context of incentive-based demand response programs. Using real-world smart meter data, we conduct a comparative analysis of the PO, SPO, and DRO methods in this context. Our results demonstrate that the SPO approach without clustering achieves the highest decision quality. In contrast, the SPO approach with temperature-based clustering yields a more favorable trade-off between decision quality and predictive accuracy, thereby better addressing both operational reliability and participant fairness.

title: Structural Characteristics and Formation Mechanisms of Patent Collaboration Networks in China's Prefabricated Buildings_supp1-3675483.pdf
authors: Min Cheng
abstract: Inter-organizational collaborative innovation is crucial for advancing the development of prefabricated buildings (PBs). However, the structural characteristics and formation mechanisms of networks arising from such collaborative innovation remain unexplored. Using patent data on PBs in China (2010-2021), this study constructed patent collaboration networks (PCNs) and examined their structural characteristics using social network analysis and network motif analysis methods. Exponential random graph models were applied to investigate the mechanisms underlying PCN formation. The results show that (i) the PCNs of PBs exhibit small-world and scale-free characteristics without a core-periphery structure, evolving from single-core to multi-core aggregation and forming an open and diverse innovation ecosystem; (ii) State Grid Corporation of China emerges as the core organization, significantly influencing knowledge flow and resource integration; (iii) enterprise-enterprise-enterprise and enterprise-enterprise-university are dominant collaborative innovation patterns for PBs. Enterprises lead PBs' innovation while universities provide technical support; (iv) PCNs' formation is driven by triadic closure, technology proximity, geography proximity, collaboration breadth, and collaboration depth, whereas organization proximity and innovation capability show inconsistent effects. Robustness tests conducted on PCNs formed by varying time intervals also support these findings. These insights provide strategic guidance for partner selection in PBs' innovation and inform policy-making for collaborative innovation initiatives.

title: Exploring the Potential of Sub-Daily Microwave Remote Sensing Observations for Estimating Evaporation in Forests
authors: Emma Tronquo, Hans Lievens, Susan C. Steele-Dunne, Niko E. C. Verhoest, Diego G. Miralles
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Terrestrial evaporation (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$E$</tex-math></inline-formula>) plays a crucial role in the water, energy, and carbon cycles and modulates climate change through multiple feedback mechanisms. While process-based models estimate <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$E$</tex-math></inline-formula> using satellite-derived drivers, they typically operate at daily or lower temporal resolutions. Key components of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$E$</tex-math></inline-formula>, such as transpiration and interception loss, exhibit strong diurnal variability, especially under water stress and during or shortly after precipitation events. Therefore, capturing the sub-daily variability of these variables is essential for improved process understanding and <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$E$</tex-math></inline-formula> monitoring at fine temporal resolutions. Sub-daily microwave observations offer the potential to resolve these short-term processes while providing all-sky retrievals. The Sub-daily Land Atmosphere INTEractions (SLAINTE) mission, proposed as part of ESA's New Earth Observation Mission Ideas, aims to provide sub-daily Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) observations of surface soil moisture (SSM), vegetation optical depth (VOD), and wet/dry canopy state (WDCS). These observations are expected to enhance the estimation of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$E$</tex-math></inline-formula> beyond current capabilities. This study explores the added value of such observations through Observing System Simulation Experiments conducted at four European eddy-covariance forest sites, constraining a sub-daily version of the Global Land Evaporation Amsterdam Model (GLEAM) with synthetic sub-daily microwave observations. Three experiments assess the impact of: (1) sub-daily SSM on bare soil evaporation and transpiration, (2) sub-daily VOD on transpiration, and (3) sub-daily WDCS on interception loss. Results demonstrate that prospective sub-daily microwave data can substantially improve <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$E$</tex-math></inline-formula> estimates and its components, showing average relative improvements in terms of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\Delta \text{RMSE}$</tex-math></inline-formula> of up to 25% for interception loss when assimilating sub-daily WDCS, and up to 33% for transpiration when using sub-daily VOD. Our results highlight the need for satellite missions that provide sub-daily microwave data to better understand forest responses to environmental stress.

title: DiffPC: Diffusion-Based Projector Photometric Compensation_supp1-3679874.pdf
authors: Yuxi Wang
abstract: Projector photometric compensation corrects color distortions introduced by surface texture, reflection, and ambient lighting. Existing deep learning-based methods usually require professional scene-specific data collection and lack consideration for perceptual quality. To address this limitation, we present a diffusion-based photometric compensation method that reconstructs compensation images under photometric and content-aware guidance. Specifically, we first model the photometric distortions introduced during projection as environment-dependent additive noise, thereby reformulating the photometric compensation problem as a denoising task with physical constraints. Next, we introduce a diffusion model, which generates compensation images by following an additive trajectory to iteratively remove the noise. Finally, to accurately estimate the noise at each timestep, by analyzing the factors that contribute to distortions in the physical process of projection and capturing, we design a noise estimation network that incorporates features of both photometry-aware and content conditions. Experiments show that our method achieves superior visual performance in unknown scenarios, thereby exhibiting significant practical advantages over prior art. Our source code is available at https://github.com/cyxwang/DiffPC.

title: Lightweight ORSI Salient Object Detection via Frequency and Mutual Assistance Attention
authors: Gongyang Li, Shixiang Shi, Yong Wu, Weisi Lin, Zhen Bai
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Lightweight Salient Object Detection in Optical Remote Sensing Image (ORSI-SOD) is expected to achieve a good balance between model complexity and detection accuracy. Existing lightweight ORSI-SOD methods usually adopt the MobileNet as the backbone, which greatly reduces the model complexity, but also restricts the detection accuracy. In this paper, we propose a novel lightweight Frequency and Mutual Assistance Attention Network, i.e., FreMaNet, with a lightweight transformer backbone for ORSI-SOD. Our FreMaNet is built on the strategy of intra-level modeling and inter-level assistance. Frequency-domain Self-Attention (FreSA) and Mutual Assistance Channel Attention (MaCA) are responsible for intra-level modeling and inter-level assistance, respectively. Specifically, FreSA is arranged behind the backbone to further model global relationships within each level of features (i.e., intra-level features). Different from the vanilla self-attention, FreSA achieves global relationship modeling through multiplication in the frequency domain, resulting in less computational load. Then, different levels of features (i.e., inter-level features) assist and interact with each other in MaCA. MaCA first performs a simple fusion on the features of two adjacent levels, and then adopts parallel self-channel attention and assistance channel attention to adaptively achieve mutual assistance of features at different levels. With the cooperation of the above components and an efficient saliency decoder, our FreMaNet has only 4.91M parameters and 4.52G floating point operations for a 352×352 input. Extensive experiments on three datasets demonstrate that our lightweight FreMaNet achieves competitive performance compared to lightweight and normal-size ORSI-SOD methods. The code and results of our method are available at https://github.com/MathLee/FreMaNet.

title: Robust Speech Emotion Recognition via Classifier Retraining on Mixup-Augmented Representations
authors: Shi-Wook Lee
publish: 2025 IEEE Automatic Speech Recognition and Understanding Workshop (ASRU)
abstract: This study proposes a novel augmentation framework for speech emotion recognition that enhances robustness and generalization by retraining the classifier on Mixupaugmented representations. We leverage the complementarity of representations from full and top fine-tuning strategies, which capture distinct emotional cues. Applying the Mixup technique across these heterogeneous representations produces interpolated representations that enrich the original representation spaces and support the learning of various and discriminative patterns. These interpolated representations are used to retrain the final classifier, enabling it to adapt to the expanded representation space and effectively integrate information from both fine-tuning perspectives. The proposed process improves the model’s ability to handle acoustic and emotional variability, leading to improved generalization under domain shifts. The method is validated on four speech emotion datasets covering two languages.

title: Success-Driven Bias-Aware Routing for Atomic Payments in Payment Channel Networks
authors: Satyajit Mohapatra, Sanjeet Kumar Nayak
publish: 2025 IEEE 32nd International Conference on High Performance Computing, Data and Analytics Workshop (HiPCW)
abstract: Payment Channel Networks (PCNs) offer high throughput transactions with negligible fees overcoming the drawbacks of blockchain. However PCNs are restricted to a fixed channel capacity. Payment Channels (PCs) might deplete if more transactions are routed through most used paths. In this work, we propose a bias-aware routing in PCNs. Here, the payments are routed in the PCN such that the balance of each PC in the network does not become skewed. We formulate an Integer Linear Programming (ILP) where we try to maximise the payment success along with minimising the bias. Initial results obtained from the simulation demonstrates that our proposed method achieves atleast 5% better success ratio and 14% less network imbalance on an average, than when targeting for only minimum fee routing. In future, we would develop heuristic mechanisms analysing the tradeoff and minimising routing fees.

title: Cryptographic Collapse Forecasting: A Strategic Shift to Quantum Security
authors: Majid K, Sreejita Chatterjee, Aayush Kumar Jha, Koteswararao Kondepu
publish: 2025 IEEE 32nd International Conference on High Performance Computing, Data and Analytics Workshop (HiPCW)
abstract: The threat of advancing quantum computers necessitates a rapid cryptographic overhaul. In response, the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) published the first finalized Post-Quantum Cryptography (PQC) standards — including ML-KEM and ML-DSA — to mitigate the urgent risk of "harvest now, decrypt later". The objective of this study is to deploy an interactive system with the Open Radio Access Network (O-RAN) by utilizing a quantum toolkit like Qiskit, in order to demonstrate the real-time breaking of classical cryptographic schemes while simultaneously validating the necessary resilience of PQC embedded systems.

title: On-the-Fly Machine Learning Interatomic Potential for Bulk PdH: Assessing Accuracy and Efficiency
authors: Poonam Parkar, Sudarshan Vijay, Abhijit Chatterjee
publish: 2025 IEEE 32nd International Conference on High Performance Computing, Data and Analytics Workshop (HiPCW)
abstract: The accurate description of palladium hydrides (PdHx, 0⩽x⩽1) is essential for understanding their role in hydrogen storage, purification and energy conversion technologies. Conventional ab initio molecular dynamics (AIMD) is prohibitively expensive for the large supercells and long time scales required to achieve statistical accuracy in capturing elastic, thermodynamic, and transport properties as well as the the complex thermodynamics and diffusion processes. In this work, we develop a machine learning force field (MLFF) for bulk PdHx using an on-the-fly active learning framework. The MLFF is fitted to data from density functional theory (DFT) in a way that guarantees accurate reproduction of interatomic forces, energies and stresses over an extensive configurational range which includes strained lattices, hydrogen concentration variations and finite-temperature behaviour. By benchmarking calculations we show that MLFF provides almost accurate results comparable to DFT, and molecular dynamics simulations can be performed in nanosecond time scale and with thousands of atoms. We demonstrate its performance in calculations of finite-temperature structural stability, volume changes, vibrational properties, and elastic constants of PdH, emphasizing both efficiency and reliability. The fully developed PdH MLFF provides a transferable and computationally efficient tool for exploring the thermomechanical behavior of metal hydrides systems.

title: MCR-TRec: A Novel Multi-Criteria Tourism Recommendation System for Tamil Nadu Using NLP
authors: Dhivya.K, Suganya R
publish: 2025 IEEE 32nd International Conference on High Performance Computing, Data and Analytics Workshop (HiPCW)
abstract: Tourism recommendation systems are pivotal for enhancing travel planning, yet they often lack personalised and contextual awareness for regional nuances. This Paper introduces MCR-TRec, a novel Multi-Criteria Tourism Recommendation system designed for Tamil Nadu. To overcome the limitations of existing systems that lack regional personalisation, MCR-TRec integrates Natural Language Processing(NLP) for understanding user queries and a novel ranking algorithm. Our key innovation is the Trace Recommendation Constraint(TRec), which dynamically incorporates real-time user location and context into multi-criteria optimisation. The system provides personalised, culturally-sensitive suggestions for Points of Interest(POIs), by integrating criteria such as user ratings, cost, proximity, and preferred category. Evaluations on a custom, real-world Tamil Nadu dataset show MCR-TRec achieves a high Accuracy of 98% and a Normalised Discounted Cumulative Gain(NDCG) of 0.94%, significantly outperforming both traditional machine learning models and advanced deep learning baselines. The demonstrates its effectiveness for intelligent, context-aware tourism planning.

title: Hybrid Foveated Path Tracing with Peripheral Gaussians for Immersive Anatomy
authors: Constantin Kleinbeck, Luisa Theelke, Hannah Schieber, Ulrich Eck, Rüdiger von Eisenhart-Rothe, Daniel Roth
publish: 2026 IEEE Conference on Virtual Reality and 3D User Interfaces (VR)
abstract: Volumetric medical imaging offers great potential for understanding complex pathologies. Yet, traditional 2D slices provide little support for interpreting spatial relationships, forcing users to mentally reconstruct anatomy into three dimensions. Direct volumetric path tracing and VR rendering can improve perception but are computationally expensive, while precomputed representations, like Gaussian Splatting, require planning ahead. Both approaches limit interactive use.We propose a hybrid rendering approach for high-quality, interactive, and immersive anatomical visualization. Our method combines streamed foveated path tracing with a lightweight Gaussian Splatting approximation of the periphery. The peripheral model generation is optimized with volume data and continuously refined using foveal renderings, enabling interactive updates. Depth-guided reprojection further improves robustness to latency and allows users to balance fidelity with refresh rate. We compare our method against direct path tracing and Gaussian Splatting. Our results highlight how their combination can preserve strengths in visual quality while re-generating the peripheral model in under a second, eliminating extensive preprocessing and approximations. This opens new options for interactive medical visualization.

title: From Speech-to-Spatial: Grounding Utterances on A Live Shared View with Augmented Reality
authors: Yoonsang Kim, Divyansh Pradhan, Devshree Jadeja, Arie E. Kaufman
publish: 2026 IEEE Conference on Virtual Reality and 3D User Interfaces (VR)
abstract: We introduce Speech-to-Spatial, a referent disambiguation framework that converts verbal remote-assistance instructions into spatially grounded AR guidance. Unlike prior systems that rely on additional cues (e.g., gesture, gaze) or manual expert annotations, Speech-to-Spatial infers the intended target solely from spoken references (speech input). Motivated by our formative study of speech referencing patterns, we characterize recurring ways people specify targets (Direct Attribute, Relational, Remembrance, and Chained) and ground them to our object-centric relational graph. Given an utterance, referent cues are parsed and rendered as persistent in-situ AR visual guidance, reducing iterative micro-guidance ("a bit more to the right", "now, stop.") during remote guidance. We demonstrate the use cases of our system with remote guided assistance and intent disambiguation scenarios. Our evaluation shows that Speech-to-Spatial improves task efficiency, reduces cognitive load, and enhances usability compared to a conventional voice-only baseline, transforming disembodied verbal instruction into visually explainable, actionable guidance on a live shared view.

title: GLFE: Video Frame Interpolation Forensics via Multi-Scale Global and Local Feature Exploitation
authors: Xiaowan Huang, Tongzhen Si, Xiaohui Yang
publish: 2025 IEEE International Workshop on Information Forensics and Security (WIFS)
abstract: With the continuous development of video technology, video frame interpolation (VFI) has made significant advances. Frame interpolation can generate new frames from existing ones to increase the frame rate. At the same time, it poses a serious threat to information security. As the videos produced by frame generation technology become increasingly realistic, many of them can mislead the public. Therefore, an algorithm that is robust and highly accurate is needed to determine whether a video is authentic. In this paper, we construct a video frame interpolation forensic framework. On one hand, we propose a multi-scale framework that processes video features from coarse to fine. On the other hand, we combine a global information module and a local information module to enrich the extracted tampering features. Additionally, we place an efficient multi-scale attention (EMA) module at the end of our framework to fuse the results from the three scales. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method achieves comparable performance to the current state-of-the-art detectors.

title: Lightweight Wasserstein Audio-Visual Model for Unified Speech Enhancement and Separation
authors: Jisoo Park, Seonghak Lee, Guisik Kim, Taewoo Kim, Junseok Kwon
publish: 2025 IEEE Automatic Speech Recognition and Understanding Workshop (ASRU)
abstract: Speech Enhancement (SE) and Speech Separation (SS) have traditionally been treated as distinct tasks in speech processing. However, real-world audio often involves both background noise and overlapping speakers, motivating the need for a unified solution. While recent approaches have attempted to integrate SE and SS within multi-stage architectures, these approaches typically involve complex, parameter-heavy models and rely on supervised training, limiting scalability and generalization. In this work, we propose UniVoiceLite, a lightweight and unsupervised audiovisual framework that unifies SE and SS within a single model. UniVoiceLite leverages lip motion and facial identity cues to guide speech extraction and employs Wasserstein distance regularization to stabilize the latent space without requiring paired noisy-clean data. Experimental results demonstrate that UniVoiceLite achieves strong performance in both noisy and multi-speaker scenarios, combining efficiency with robust generalization. The source code is available at https://github.com/jisoo-o/UniVoiceLite.

title: CAMÕES: A Comprehensive Automatic Speech Recognition Benchmark for European Portuguese
authors: Carlos Carvalho, Francisco Teixeira, Catarina Botelho, Anna Pompili, Rubén Solera-Ureña, Sérgio Paulo, Mariana Julião, Thomas Rolland, John Mendonça, Diogo Pereira, Isabel Trancoso, Alberto Abad
publish: 2025 IEEE Automatic Speech Recognition and Understanding Workshop (ASRU)
abstract: Existing resources for Automatic Speech Recognition in Portuguese are mostly focused on Brazilian Portuguese, leaving European Portuguese (EP) and other varieties underexplored. To bridge this gap, we introduce CAMÕES, the first open framework for EP and other Portuguese varieties. It consists of (1) a comprehensive evaluation benchmark, including 46 h of EP test data spanning multiple domains; and (2) a collection of state-of-the-art models. For the latter, we consider multiple foundation models, evaluating their zero-shot and fine-tuned performances, as well as E-Branchformer models trained from scratch. A curated set of $\mathbf{4 2 5 h}$ of EP was used for both fine-tuning and training. Our results show comparable performance for EP between fine-tuned foundation models and the E-Branchformer. Furthermore, the best-performing models achieve relative improvements above 35% WER, compared to the strongest zero-shot foundation model, establishing a new state-of-the-art for EP and other varieties.

title: Is Smaller Always Faster? Tradeoffs in Compressing Self-Supervised Speech Transformers
authors: Tzu-Quan Lin, Tsung-Huan Yang, Chun-Yao Chang, Kuang-Ming Chen, Tzu-Hsun Feng, Hung-Yi Lee, Hao Tang
publish: 2025 IEEE Automatic Speech Recognition and Understanding Workshop (ASRU)
abstract: Transformer-based self-supervised models have achieved remarkable success in speech processing, but their large size and high inference cost present significant challenges for real-world deployment. While numerous compression techniques have been proposed, inconsistent evaluation metrics make it difficult to compare their practical effectiveness. In this work, we conduct a comprehensive study of four common compression methods, including weight pruning, head pruning, low-rank approximation, and knowledge distillation on self-supervised speech Transformers. We evaluate each method under three key metrics: parameter count, multiply-accumulate operations, and real-time factor. Results show that each method offers distinct advantages. In addition, we contextualize recent compression techniques, comparing DistilHuBERT, FitHuBERT, LightHuBERT, ARMHuBERT, and STaRHuBERT under the same framework, offering practical guidance on compression for deployment.

title: Thoth: Uncovering Data-Dependent Memory Access Patterns via Annotation-Directed Load Sampling
authors: Kanheng Jiang, Yongxin Lyu, Zhiyuan Zhang, Zengshi Wang, Chao Fu, Jun Han
publish: ACM Transactions on Architecture and Code Optimization
abstract: None

title: Exploring Student Perceptions on Integrating Multi-Modality in GenAI
authors: Shile Zhang, Shuhan Zhang, Weijian Liang
abstract: The integration of Generative AI (GenAI) into education has sparked interest in its potential to enhance learning efficiency. However, debates persist regarding its effects on students' learning experience and satisfaction. Additionally, challenges, risks, and the role of multi-modality in improving user experience remain underexplored. To address these gaps, we conducted a survey of 202 students (aged 14–23) from two vocational schools and three universities in southern China. The analysis of the survey results validated the User Interaction with Chatbot Interfaces (UICI) questionnaire as a reliable tool and demonstrated that multi-modality—through visual or audio content—can help mitigate challenges in specific scenarios, thereby enhancing student experiences. Five user categories emerged from the findings: Adaptation Optimization (AO), Risk Mitigation (RM), Interest Stimulation (IS), Scenario Support (SS), and Feature Expansion (FE). These insights provide actionable guidance for designing more effective and inclusive educational GenAI tools.

title: NeRF Reconstruction Quality Under Various Image Artifacts
authors: Alexander Xiang
abstract: Neural Radiance Fields (NeRF) is a method used to generate novel views of complex 3D scenes by using a neural network to train a continuous 3D representation from a series of 2D images taken from different viewpoints. One of the key concepts to render these scenes involves ray integration, as the NeRF algorithm assumes that the color of each pixel in an image results from the linear integration of reflected light rays from all directions, with the intensity being directly proportional to the amount of light received. For each pixel in each input photo provided to the NeRF, the neural network estimates the camera’s position and casts a ray through the pixel along its path. At each sample points along, the network predicts the color and volume density. These values are then linearly integrated along the ray to determine the final color represented in the reconstructed scene. However, this could be a problem for real world CMOS/CCD urban camera sensors, where illumination can be potentially very bright or saturated. Even without saturation, many sensors utilize non-linear gamma correction to measure sensor response, which might be problematic for the linear integration. In working with the WRIVA program at JHU APL, we want to ask an important question: Does the non-linear response from gamma correction break the physics of NeRF so the traditional integration method won’t work anymore?

title: Continuous representation of tactile information by an unsupervised liquid state machine
authors: Katsunori Kitano, Mitsuhito Ando, Haruo Noma
abstract: Tactile information is indispensable for real-world systems such as robots used for manipulating physical objects. Tactile sensation is acquired as a time-series information by mechano-transduction systems, and the system responsible for processing such inputs must effectively process the temporal structure in these acquired signals. However, the implementation of tactile perception remains challenging. Furthermore, because supervised learning is unrealistic for real-world sensory perception systems, understanding the role of unsupervised learning in perception is crucial. In this study, we proposed a liquid state machine with unsupervised learning for tactile perception and examined the mechanism by which it discriminated signals from different materials. The model encoded tactile information into the spatio-temporal dynamics of readout neurons based on texture smoothness. Most sensory perception systems use supervised learning; consequently, their response to unknown inputs remains unclear. The proposed system can predict which known material resembles an unknown input. This feature is essential as real-world systems encounter unfamiliar information and environments.

title: Long-Term Household Electricity Consumption Forecasting via Ensemble Learning Approach: Application of the TreeBagger Model
authors: F. Issi
abstract: Accurate long-horizon electricity consumption forecasting is essential for effective energy management, operational planning, and grid stability. This study proposes a TreeBagger-based ensemble learning framework for monthly residential electricity consumption prediction using an 88-month dataset. The proposed methodology incorporates seasonal memory through lagged consumption variables, cyclic month encoding, and trend components to capture both short-term autoregressive patterns and annual seasonality. To ensure a rigorous evaluation, the proposed model was compared with several baseline forecasting approaches, including ARIMA, Support Vector Regression (SVR), and XGBoost, under a fixed-origin training–testing framework. Experimental results show that the TreeBagger model achieves the best overall predictive performance, producing the lowest RMSE (30.582) and MAPE (15.96%) among the evaluated models, while maintaining competitive MAE values. The findings demonstrate that bagging-based ensemble models are highly effective for modeling the nonlinear and seasonal characteristics of household electricity consumption data. The proposed approach provides a reliable and computationally efficient framework for medium-scale monthly electricity demand forecasting and can support practical applications such as energy planning and consumption analysis.

title: Hybrid Coordination Framework for Centralized Task Allocation and Execution in Heterogeneous Multi-Robot Systems
authors: T. Glace Varghese, Sreeja Kochuvila, Navin Kumar, R. R. Venkatesha Prasad
abstract: Multi-robot coordination has emerged as a critical enabler for efficient warehouse logistics, yet existing approaches struggle to balance solution quality, computational efficiency, and real-time adaptability in heterogeneous robot environments. This paper presents a novel centralized coordination architecture that integrates a hybrid optimization algorithm combining the shapley value clustering algorithm (SVCA) with the non-dominated sorting genetic algorithm II (NSGA-II) for multi-robot task allocation (MRTA). The proposed system addresses the complex challenge of allocating logistics tasks to heterogeneous mobile robots with varying payload capacities, energy consumption rates, and task deadline while simultaneously optimizing multiple conflicting objectives including makespan, energy consumption, deadline adherence, and workload balance. Unlike traditional static scheduling methods, the proposed system includes dynamic task reallocation capabilities that enable robots to autonomously detect and execute pending tasks nearby upon completion, be it with intelligent battery management that triggers autonomous recharging when energy thresholds are reached. Implemented using robot operating system 2 (ROS2) Humble and validated in Gazebo simulation environments with warehouse scenarios involve five heterogeneous robots and multiple logistics tasks. Experimental results show that, with the hybrid algorithm, 100% task allocation can be achieved with an average allocation time of 0.122 seconds, significantly outperforming state-of-the-art methods including island model genetic algorithm (IMGA), SVCA, particle swarm optimization (PSO), genetic algorithm (GA), and standalone NSGA-II executions. The system effectively manages complex logistics operations to automate thewarehouse within the simulated environment, while maintaining required constraint.

title: Active Versus Passive: CRB Constrained Beamforming Design and Reflection Manipulation for RIS Assisted ISAC System
authors: Zhongyu Ma, Xinji Hu, Zhaobin Li, Guangjie Han, Yingting Liu
abstract: This letter investigates the joint design of beamforming and reflection manipulation for the active reconfigurable intelligent surface-assisted integrated sensing and communication (ARIS-ISAC) system, where multi-user communication and sensing tasks are supported by an active RIS and a base station (BS). The sum rate maximization problem is formulated as a complex non-convex optimization problem, subject to constraints on the Cramér-Rao bound (CRB) for sensing accuracy, BS transmit power, and active RIS reflection coefficients. A hybrid approach combining fractional programming (FP), majorization-minimization (MM) and block coordinate descent (BCD) is proposed to solve the decoupled sub-problems iteratively. Simulation results demonstrate that ARIS-ISAC achieves more efficient collaboration between high-precision sensing and reliable communication than its passive counterparts.

title: IEEE Women in Engineering
authors: N/A
publish: IEEE Transactions on Terahertz Science and Technology

title: EdgeEnhance-YOLO: A Lightweight Small Object Detection Model with Multi-Dimensional Edge Enhancement
authors: Na Liu, Weixuan Kong, Yuanqin Jiang, Wankun Shan, Hui Xiao, Xuefeng Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: Small object detection faces multiple challenges, including high-frequency information degradation, complex back ground interference, and low target resolution, which frequently lead to missed and false detections. To address these issues, this paper proposes an end-to-end multidimensional edge-enhanced detection network, named EE-YOLO, which exhibits significant technical innovations in structural design and feature fusion mechanisms. Firstly, an Edge-Enhanced Convolution module (EEConv) is designed with a dual-branch parallel architecture, enabling the synchronous extraction of spatial information and fine-grained edge details in shallow network layers, thereby effectively mitigat ing high-frequency information loss. Secondly, an Edge-Enhanced Multi-Scale Feature Fusion Framework (EEMFF) is developed to overcome the limitations of conventional feature pyramid structures. Through a direct edge injection mechanism, high-resolution shallow edge features are fused across layers into deep semantic representations, while a four-scale fusion module provides enriched contextual information to enhance small-object detection capability globally. Finally, to balance detection accuracy and computational efficiency under resource-constrained conditions, a Detail-Enhance Lightweight Shared Convolution Detection Head (DELSCD) is constructed, which maintains high-precision detection while sig nificantly reducing model parameters. Extensive experiments con ducted on the VisDrone2019, TinyPerson and RS-STOD datasets demonstrate that EE-YOLO, with fewer than 10M parameters, surpasses existing publicly available models, achieving 51.0% and 33.5% mAP(50) and mAP(50-95) on VisDrone2019, 30.9% and 12.0% on TinyPerson, and 83.2% and 58.8% on RS-STOD, respectively. The research results achieve both technical breakthroughs in lightweight small object detection and engineering advances for real-world visual perception tasks, offering an innovative solution that effectively balances detection accuracy and deployment efficiency. The proposed EE-YOLO framework demonstrates strong scalability and applicability across a wide range of practical scenarios, including intelligent transportation monitoring, public safety monitoring, and industrial inspection environments.

title: Josephson Junction Radiators as Spectral Calibrators of Millimeter-Wave Low-Temperature Detectors
authors: Johannes Hubmayr, Shannon M. Duff, Robert McDermott, Corwin Shiu, Gabriel Spahn, David Harrison, Joel N. Ullom, Anna Vaskuri
abstract: The Josephson junction radiator (JJR) is a thin-film superconducting device that converts DC voltage into coherent millimeter-wave (mm-wave) radiation. The source is frequency-tunable, instantaneously narrow-band, compact, lightweight, has no moving parts, and may share the same cryogenic and vacuum space as the device under test. With these favorable characteristics, the JJR may find use as a general purpose calibration tool for low temperature mm-wave detectors. We show that the JJR unambiguously emits mm-wave radiation and couples to a mm-wave detector. We use the JJR to determine the spectral response of a superconducting mm-wave detector that operates in two large frequency passbands centered at 150 GHz and 220 GHz. A comparison to measurements with a conventional Fourier Transform Spectrometer is presented, and the use of the JJR as a precision spectral calibration tool is discussed.

title: One-bit Single-Measurement Spectral Compressed Sensing via Hankel Matrix Factorization
authors: Xu Zhang, Chang Gao, Qing Cai, Zhao Wang, Jinsheng Li, Maoguo Gong
abstract: This paper studies the problem of recovering a spectrally sparse signal from its one-bit single-measurement vector. Most previous methods depend on multiple measurement vectors, which face inherent challenges in real-time critical scenarios. Regarding the single-measurement vector, only a few on-grid approaches have been proposed; however, these techniques suffer from estimation errors due to grid mismatch. To facilitate gridless reconstruction under a one-bit single-measurement vector, a novel optimization model is devised by leveraging both the low-rank property of the lifting Hankel matrix and the sign consistency between the target signal and its estimate. Consequently, a onebit spectral compressed sensing algorithm via Hankel matrix factorization, termed OSCAR, is proposed by incorporating a one-sided ℓp norm smoothing technique. Furthermore, an accelerated OSCAR algorithm, named Acc-OSCAR, is developed by integrating momentum techniques to expedite convergence. Theoretical results provide the convergence analysis for the proposed algorithms. Numerical results confirm the excellent performance of the proposed algorithms.

title: Co-Creating Intelligent Systems to Improve Urban Mobility for Visually Impaired People, From the Perspective of a Visually Impaired Researcher
authors: Peter A Hayton
abstract: None

title: Reputation-Based Hyperledger Fabric for Private and Scalable Data Sharing in CAVs
authors: Rahma Hammedi, Devki Nandan Jha, David J Brown, Mohammad Aljaidi, Yue Cao, Omprakash Kaiwartya
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Vehicular Technology
abstract: The operation of Connected and Autonomous Vehicles (CAVs) is primarily driven by the continuous exchange of data from various sources, including in-vehicle sensors, neighbouring vehicles, and roadside infrastructure. This continuous data exchange results in the accumulation of large volumes of data with dimensionality, which is essential for accurate decision-making in autonomous driving functions. Shared data often encompasses highly sensitive data such as precise vehicular location, driver identification and behavioural patterns. As a result, there is an increasing public concern over the privacy implications associated with the extensive data exchanges. In this context, this research investigates access control mechanisms that ensure privacy and trust in CAVs. We propose a Reputation-based Hyperledger Fabric (RepHLF) framework, a novel privacy-preserving architecture that integrates across-channel Hyperledger Fabric blockchain with a dynamic, multi-metric reputation model for CAV environments. The reputation mechanism evaluates vehicles based on three parameters: behavioural integrity, legitimacy, and historical communication reliability. Each parameter evolves using exponential decay functions to reflect temporal relevance. Data access is subsequently restricted to trusted vehicles only, according to their computed reputation index. The performance of RepHLF is evaluated in terms of accuracy, latency, memory usage, privacy loss, and communication overhead using Hyperledger Caliper. Simulation results demonstrate ultra-low average latency of approximately 201 ms, minimal communication cost of 2.8 KB per transaction, Throughput of 4.99 TPS and high learning accuracy of 99.94%, while maintaining a bounded privacy loss of 3.12%. The integrated reputation mechanism within the consensus algorithm further enhances network reliability by dynamically identifying untrusted vehicles and restricting their transaction requests. Further analysis of the proposed model, RepHLF, against existing reputation-based models demonstrates superior dynamic trust evolution and robust privacy preservation.

title: SoMDet3D: Collaborative 3D Object Detection Via Synesthesia of Machines
authors: Zengle Zhu, Rongqing Zhang, Xiang Cheng, Liuqing Yang
abstract: With the development of intelligent transportation systems, vehicles increasingly support intelligent applications via advanced technologies. To ensure their safety, significant research is devoted to improving the environmental perception capabilities. A key focus is ensuring that vehicles can accurately detect objects in various road environments. However, complex road scenarios and variations in weather conditions pose significant challenges. Considering the limitations of a single sensor and the restricted field of view of a single vehicle, a feasible approach is to establish a multi-sensor Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X) collaborative perception environment based on heterogeneous data from multiple agents. However, data heterogeneity and bandwidth limitations hinder the ability of vehicles to collaborate effectively. As a solution, Synesthesia of Machines (SoM) provides a robust framework for fusing multi-modal and multi-agent data. In this paper, we propose a collaborative 3D object detection model via SoM (SoMDet3D). SoMDet3D effectively fuses multi-modal features through the sampling mechanism and channel attention. These features from other agents are compressed and transmitted to the ego autonomous vehicle, where a heterogeneous spatial transformer aggregates the data. Experimental results on the V2XSet dataset show that SoMDet3D outperforms existing benchmarks in terms of both performance and communication overhead.
