title: A 2 m resolution impervious surface distribution product of the Qinghai-Xizang Plateau based on SegformerAT model
authors: Kaiyuan Zheng, Guojin He, Ranyu Yin, Guizhou Wang, Tengfei Long, Zhaoming Zhang
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: High-resolution impervious surface data is essential for studies related to urban development, human activities, carbon emissions, and ecological environments. Owing to the unique geographic and climatic conditions, impervious surfaces in the Qinghai-Xizang (Tibet) Plateau (QXP) are often surrounded by sparsely vegetated areas with low canopy cover and high land exposure. Additionally, the QXP is characterized by numerous, scattered small-scale impervious surfaces (SSIS). Existing impervious surface products, primarily at global or national scales, typically provide medium spatial resolutions (30-m or 10-m) and exhibit significant omission and commission errors for impervious surfaces in QXP, particularly in mapping SSIS. Consequently, a 2 m impervious surface distribution product for QXP in 2020 (QXP-ISD2m product) was created using Gaofen imagery. This product categorizes roads as a separate class to enhance the data's applicability and defines non-road impervious surfaces as Primary Impervious Surfaces (PIS). Specifically, a framework for large-scale impervious surface mapping was proposed. Initially, the QXP 2 m Impervious Surface Unique Dataset, consisting of 2235 samples, was constructed using a target-scale-based stratified sampling strategy and augmented to 4807 samples by incorporating spatially overlapping multi-temporal and multi-sensor images (2 m resolution, 512× 512 pixels). This dataset accommodates various scales of impervious surface types in the QXP and ensures accurate impervious surface extraction. To address significant scale discrepancies between PIS and road targets, an improved Segformer model, named SegformerAT, was introduced. This model employs patch-wise normalization and utilizes the augmented dataset for training, thus ensuring high accuracy in PIS extraction and significantly improving road detection accuracy. Finally, the Posterior-Knowledge-Based Merging Rule (PKBMR) was employed to achieve effective large-scale mapping. Validation of the QXP-ISD2m product with a set of 7000 independent samples yielded F1 scores of 0.894 for PIS and 0.895 for roads. Comparative experiments with other algorithms further confirmed the superiority of the SegformerAT model.

title: Comparing Spatial AR to Passthrough Headset AR in a Real Virtual Heritage Experience_supp1-3679922.mp4
authors: Narcis Pares

title: A PINN-Based Controller for Dual-Plane Voltage Predictive Control of Five-Phase PMSMs
authors: Ke Li, Jiakai Wang, Xiaodong Sun, Xuhao Zha, Zonghao Su, Gang Lei, Jianguo Zhu
abstract: This article proposes a novel physics-informed neural network (PINN) controller for five-phase permanent magnet synchronous motors (PMSMs) that addresses the limitations of existing dual-plane current control methods. The notable constraints of existing machine learning controllers for PMSMs, particularly with respect to interpretability and stability, are examined to enable offline interpretability and online deployability. A neural network (NN) control model is derived for dual-plane operation of five-phase PMSMs by extending the NN control frameworks of three-phase PMSMs. The motor’s prior physical information is deeply integrated into the training process to enhance generalization. A hybrid optimization strategy is proposed to improve the fit between the predicted and actual operating voltages and to suppress prediction fluctuations, thereby better aligning the predicted values with the exact operational requirements. Experimental results validate the excellent steady-state performance and robust antiinterference performance of the proposed controller.

title: Joint ASR and Speech Attribute Prediction for Conversational Dysarthric Speech Analysis with Multimodal Language Models
authors: Dominik Wagner, Ilja Baumann, Natalie Engert, Elmar Nöth, Korbinian Riedhammer, Tobias Bocklet
publish: 2025 IEEE Automatic Speech Recognition and Understanding Workshop (ASRU)
abstract: Dysarthric speech recognition systems often focus solely on transcription, limiting their applicability in clinical and assistive settings where assessments of perceptual attributes like intelligibility and naturalness are essential. We propose a multimodal conversational framework based on Phi-4-Multimodal that combines ASR with attribute rating prediction, enabling users to query both transcriptions and perceptual characteristics (e.g. “How intelligible is this utterance?”). Our multitask model adds auxiliary prediction heads for five clinically relevant attributes and is trained on the English Speech Accessibility Project dataset. The system achieves competitive ASR performance while delivering attribute-level feedback comparable to specialized classifiers. Additional experiments show improved ASR performance for German Parkinson’s speech, indicating preserved multilingual capabilities and partial cross-lingual transfer of dysarthric speech patterns.

title: Inference-aware Node Allocation to Reduce HPC Performance Variability
authors: Arshit Narang, Ruthvik Tunuguntla, Pratham Sahu, Preeti Malakar
publish: 2025 IEEE 32nd International Conference on High Performance Computing, Data and Analytics Workshop (HiPCW)
abstract: Motivation: High Performance Computing (HPC) systems are foundational to scientific discovery. Despite advances in hardware and interconnect technologies, application performance variability remains a significant challenge. Identical workloads often exhibit different runtimes due to I/O contention, network congestion, and shared resource interference. Contemporary job schedulers do not consider the interference between jobs executing on shared switches. This may lead to increased runtimes and increased performance variability [1] in case of jobs with similar communication and I/O patterns. Thus it is necessary for job schedulers to consider job interference and job I/O characteristics during node allocation. The objective of this work is to reduce performance variability in managed clusters and supercomputers. We propose two complementary job-aware scheduling approaches – (1) a mutual information (MI) based job interference-aware allocation and (2) a CNN-driven I/O-aware allocation strategy.

title: GPU-Accelerated Neural ODEs for Stiff Dynamical Systems Using CVODES
authors: Ved Vartak, Subhajit Sanfui, Ramsatish Kaluri
publish: 2025 IEEE 32nd International Conference on High Performance Computing, Data and Analytics Workshop (HiPCW)
abstract: Neural ordinary differential equations (ODEs) are a family of neural networks where the hidden states are evolved by integrating time-dependent derivatives representing the system behavior. They have been successfully applied in various applications like dynamical systems, optimal estimation and process control. One of the key challenges in neural ODEs is the unavailability of an efficient GPU-accelerated implicit ODE solver with gradient calculation support for stiff systems in a Python-based framework. Our work involves addressing this challenge by implementing a Python library wrapping around SUNDIALS CVODES, an existing C-based state-of-art ODE solver library and enabling GPU acceleration by implementing support for PyTorch tensors. Through the implemented library, we have introduced a fast GPU accelerated implicit ODE solver to PyTorch framework allowing use of neural ODEs on stiff dynamical systems. We validate and benchmark this implementation with a state-of-art library Diffrax and demonstrate significantly faster training on GPU for stiff systems.

title: A GPU is All You Need: Rethinking Distributed and Out-of-Core GNN Training
authors: Pranjal Naman, Yogesh Simmhan
publish: 2025 IEEE 32nd International Conference on High Performance Computing, Data and Analytics Workshop (HiPCW)
abstract: Training Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) on large graphs is typically done on complex, distributed, or Out-of-Core (OOC) systems due to memory pressure. But we challenge this assumption as training typically requires access to only a small subset of node features. As a result, training on many large graphs exhibits relatively small working sets that can fit on a modest GPU. We present a simple single-GPU framework that pre-computes computation graphs and feature subsets to eliminate CPU–GPU data transfers and runtime overheads. Our approach achieves reductions in epoch time of up to 45× over the distributed DistDGL framework and ≈ 2× over the OOC DiskGNN framework, demonstrating that a modern GPU is all you need for efficient large-scale GNN training.

title: A Survey and Evaluation Framework for Secure DNS Resolution
authors: Ali Sadeghi Jahromi, AbdelRahman Abdou, Paul C. van Oorschot
abstract: Since security was not among the original design goals of the Domain Name System (herein called Vanilla DNS), many secure DNS schemes have been proposed to enhance the security and privacy of the DNS resolution process. Some proposed schemes aim to replace the existing DNS infrastructure entirely, but none have succeeded in doing so. In parallel, numerous schemes focus on improving DNS security without modifying its fundamental two-stage structure. These efforts highlight the feasibility of addressing DNS security as two distinct but compatible stages. We survey DNS resolution process attacks and threats and develop a comprehensive threat model and attack taxonomy for their systematic categorization. This analysis results in the formulation of 14 desirable security, privacy, and availability properties to mitigate the identified threats. Using these properties, we develop an objective evaluation framework and apply it to comparatively analyze 14 secure DNS schemes surveyed in this work that aim to augment the properties of the DNS resolution process. Our evaluation reveals that no single scheme provides ideal protection across the entire resolution path. Instead, the schemes tend to address a subset of properties specific to individual stages. Since these schemes target different stages of DNS resolution and are complementary, combining compatible schemes offers a practical and effective approach to achieving comprehensive security in the DNS resolution process.

title: Reallocation of Body Ownership Between Avatar and Omniscient Entity in Third-Person Perspective VR
authors: Jiayi Hu, Kai Guo, Zhongrui Kang, Yuki Ban, Shin’Ichi Warisawa
publish: 2026 IEEE Conference on Virtual Reality and 3D User Interfaces (VR)
abstract: Third-person perspective (3PP) VR can afford dual embodiment, letting users act through an in-world avatar while simultaneously operating an omniscient entity (OE) from a detached viewpoint. We investigate how the sense of body ownership (SoBO) reallocates between these two asymmetric entities during continuous play. We systematically varied macro-level design factors—avatar and OE involvement—across three maze scenes and one combat scene in a within-subjects study. After each trial, participants produced time-aligned, continuous SoBO traces for both entities; gaze and controller signals were logged, and recall interviews captured first-person explanations. This research reveals three main findings. First, SoBO is not statically fixed but dynamically reallocated between the avatar and the OE across task phases. Second, allocation follows design factors: higher OE involvement in the maze strengthened ownership toward it, while combat consistently favored the avatar. Third, moment-to-moment gaze and control intensity predict ownership allocation, with fluency and salient events emerging as additional influences. These findings provide a framework for understanding dynamic SoBO and inform the design of adaptive 3PP VR systems.

title: FlexiCamAR: Enhancing Everyday Camera Interactions on AR Glasses with a Flexible Additional Viewpoint
authors: Ziming Li, Hongji Li, Jialin Wang, Pan Hui, Hai-Ning Liang
publish: 2026 IEEE Conference on Virtual Reality and 3D User Interfaces (VR)
abstract: The recent emergence and popularity of consumer-grade augmented reality (AR) glasses from major technology companies highlight their potential to become the next daily computing platform. A dominant design trend in this context is the integration of a front-facing camera to deliver a first-person perspective. While this approach is intuitive, there is limited evidence that it is optimal (or sufficient) for supporting users in daily tasks. This paper explores a more effective camera interaction technique for AR glasses, which we term "FlexiCamAR". This novel method aims to enhance both efficiency and the range of applications for AR glasses by offering flexible and comfortable secondary camera viewpoints. To investigate the applicability and usability of this approach, we developed a ring camera prototype that can be attached to users’ fingers. We then conducted a user study with 12 participants, comparing FlexiCamAR against the baseline, a traditional front-facing AR camera setup, across two common tasks: taking photos and scanning QR codes. Our findings show that FlexiCamAR significantly reduces physical load. We also explore potential scenarios where the additional viewpoint afforded by FlexiCamAR proves valuable, such as capturing low-angle perspectives or navigating confined spaces. Participant feedback further suggests strong potential for additional applications, including selfie taking, video conferencing, and object scanning. Overall, FlexiCamAR presents a novel interaction approach that can serve as a powerful supplement or alternative to the first-person perspective, significantly improving the adaptability of AR glasses for everyday use.

title: Aligning Realities: A Registration Pipeline for Arbitrary Objects in Mixed Reality Using Controller-Based Point Selection
authors: David Mertens, Steffen-Sascha Stein, Kristoffer Waldow, Arnulph Fuhrmann
publish: 2026 IEEE Conference on Virtual Reality and 3D User Interfaces (VR)
abstract: A wide range of Mixed Reality (MR) applications rely on haptic interaction with real-world objects. These interactions range from grasping and standing on objects, to writing on their surfaces. Accurate alignment of a real-world object with its virtual counterpart enables users to perceive and interact with it both visually and haptically. However, existing registration methods often rely on tracked, external markers, are geometrically constrained, or require complex setups that limit their practical applicability. Hence, we present an integrated pipeline for rapid and accurate registration of arbitrary physical objects in MR. The proposed approach combines custom 3D-printed add-ons for Meta Quest 3 controllers with a point-to-point matching algorithm based on Kabsch optimization, enabling precise selection, calibration, and registration. The method is objectively evaluated using a modern motion capture rig against other state-of-the-art registration techniques. An additional user-centric evaluation helps to understand the general usability of our pipeline. Source code and datasets are released as open resources to encourage reproducibility and further research.

title: Quantitative Evaluation of Hazard Mitigation Strategies in a 5-Stage RISC-V Pipeline: A Benchmark-Driven Analysis Using Fibonacci and Prime Sieve
authors: Kushagra Agrawal, Anindita Basak, Abhishek Raj, Aakarsh Singh
publish: 2025 IEEE 32nd International Conference on High Performance Computing, Data and Analytics Workshop (HiPCW)
abstract: This paper presents a quantitative deep dive into the hazard mitigation strategies of a custom five-stage (IF–ID–EX–MEM–WB) pipelined RISC-V processor. The architecture was built with a dedicated hazard detection unit and full data forwarding to effectively handle Read-After-Write (RAW) dependencies and control flow challenges. To evaluate performance, we conducted cycle-accurate simulations using targeted benchmarks: the Fibonacci sequence to stress data forwarding and the Prime Number Sieve to assess branch handling efficiency.The results from rigorous testing showcase the architecture’s robustness. Complete success in hazard mitigation was confirmed by recording zero data stalls across all workloads, demonstrating perfect resolution of RAW dependencies. The processor achieved an efficient Cycles Per Instruction (CPI) of 1.62 on computationally intensive tasks. Even for the branch-heavy Prime Sieve, it maintained a predictable CPI of 2.90. This performance reflects a precise one-to-one correlation between branch instructions and stalls, indicating a deterministic one-cycle branch penalty. These findings validate a sound architectural design and highlight the processor’s suitability for educational platforms and embedded systems where predictable performance is essential.

title: Towards Building Trustworthy Data Provenance for Agentic Workflows
authors: Aishwarya R Parab, Prakhar Pradhan, Manit Tanwar, Yogesh Simmhan, Arnab K. Paul
publish: 2025 IEEE 32nd International Conference on High Performance Computing, Data and Analytics Workshop (HiPCW)
abstract: Agentic workflows [1] , where autonomous agents plan, coordinate, and execute complex tasks, are an emerging approach for AI-driven systems. These workflows enable smooth data transfer between agents, dynamic task distribution, and scalable decision-making. However, there are issues with reproducibility, transparency, and trust when such workflows are used in critical applications. It becomes difficult to validate results or ensure accountability when users cannot see how data moves through agents and how decisions are made.

title: Efficient methods for ab initio modelling of materials using finite-element DFT with meta-GGA functionals
authors: Srinibas Nandi, Phani Motamarri
publish: 2025 IEEE 32nd International Conference on High Performance Computing, Data and Analytics Workshop (HiPCW)
abstract: Over the past thirty years, ab initio modelling of materials using quantum mechanical theories has garnered significant attention in materials modelling. Among these, density functional theory (DFT), particularly the formulation introduced by Kohn and Sham, has emerged as the predominant approach for quantum modeling materials. Substantial increase in computing power over the past few decades has made it possible to accurately predict the electronic, optical, and mechanical properties of a diverse array of materials using DFT simulations. However, the computational cost of DFT, which scales cubically with the number of electrons due to the repeated diagonalization of the Hamiltonian matrix, has limited its applicability to systems comprising no more than a few hundred atoms. Exploiting modern parallel and heterogeneous architectures requires algorithms designed for extreme concurrency without compromising accuracy. Common choices of basis sets, plane waves (PW) and atomic orbitals (AO), have inevitable shortcomings: PWs are restricted to periodic systems, impose uniform resolution (inefficient for nonperiodic clusters or localized defects), and do not support general boundary conditions; AOs lack systematic convergence and can be unreliable for metallic or heterogeneous systems. Furthermore, both PW and AO basis sets exhibit poor scalability on supercomputing architectures due to their spatially extended nature, rendering them unsuitable for large-scale DFT simulations. To tackle these challenges, it is essential to develop methodologies that are robust, efficient, accurate, and scalable, all while taking into account the capabilities and constraints of modern heterogeneous architectures.

title: Meta Audiobox Aesthetics: Unified Automatic Assessment for Speech, Music and Sound
authors: Andros Tjandra, Yi-Chiao Wu, Baishan Guo, John Hoffman, Brian Ellis, Apoorv Vyas, Bowen Shi, Sanyuan Chen, Matt Le, Nick Zacharov, Carleigh Wood, Ann Lee, Wei-Ning Hsu
publish: 2025 IEEE Automatic Speech Recognition and Understanding Workshop (ASRU)
abstract: Quantifying audio aesthetics is challenging due to its subjective nature, influenced by human perception and cultural context. Traditional methods rely on human listeners, leading to inconsistencies and high resource demands. This paper addresses the growing need for automated systems capable of predicting audio aesthetics without human intervention. Such systems are crucial for applications like data filtering, pseudo-labeling, and evaluating generative models.In this paper, we propose new annotation guidelines that break down human listening perspectives into four axes and develop no-reference, peritem prediction models for more nuanced audio quality assessment. Our models are evaluated against human mean opinion scores (MOS) and existing methods, demonstrating comparable or superior performance. This research not only advances the field of audio aesthetics but also provides open-source models and datasets to facilitate future work and benchmarking.

title: Frequency Tunable Impedance Transformer based on Magnetostatic Wave Resonator
authors: Chin-Yu Chang, Shun Yao, Xingyu Du, Shuxian Wu, Roy H. Olsson
abstract: In this work, a frequency-tunable passive impedance transformer based on a microfabricated magnetostatic spin-wave resonator is demonstrated for wake-up receiver (WuRX) front-ends in ultra-low-power Internet of Things (IoT) sensor nodes. The device is fabricated using a 3 μm yttrium iron garnet (YIG) thin-film on a gadolinium gallium garnet (GGG) substrate and operates in a magnetostatic surface-wave (MSSW) mode whose resonant frequency is tunable via applied magnetic field. An imbalanced port impedance design enhances the intrinsic impedance transformation capability, enabling high passive voltage gain between the antenna and the envelope detector. Simulated passive gain results based on measured frequency responses show a voltage gain ranging from 16.1 dB to 10 dB as the frequency is tuned from 3.5 GHz to 9.1 GHz. Moreover, the proposed YIG impedance transformer maintains stable gain over a wide range of load capacitances, offering a magnetically reconfigurable and load-tolerant impedance transformer for next-generation WuRX front-ends.

title: Stable On-Chip MLC RRAM with a Bitline Regulation-Free Analog MAC Scheme for High-Efficiency In-Memory Computing
authors: Zhen Kong, Jun Lan, Humiao Li, Zhengke Yang, Weirong Dong, Junjie He, Horan Lyu, Xuye Wang, Yingjie Zhu, Saurabh Jain, Xiao Huang, Zhixiong Li, Jiamin Li, Feichi Zhou, Yida Li, Longyang Lin
abstract: Multi-level-cell (MLC) resistive random-access memory (RRAM) promises superior computational density and energy efficiency for compute-in-memory (CIM) systems, yet its potential is limited by analog RRAM instability and the power-hungry bitline (BL) regulation amplifiers in prior Ohm's-law-based designs. We demonstrate a 180-nm CIM macro with process-optimized back-end-of-line (BEOL) HfO2 MLC RRAM. The devices exhibit stable 3-bit conductance states (<9.8% deviation), 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">4</sup>-cycle endurance, and 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">4</sup>-s retention at 85°C. At the circuit level, a BL regulation-free analog MAC scheme combined with a bit-masked multi-bit input method eliminates column-wise BL-regulation amplifiers and input-bit-significance recovery. This enables direct-current-free computation, reducing per-column power by >74%. Silicon measurements of the 180-nm prototype deliver a peak energy (area) efficiency of 819 TOPS/W/bit (1.115 TOPS/mm²/bit). Furthermore, post-layout simulations of a 22-nm implementation utilizing extracted RC parasitics and a Verilog-A RRAM model calibrated to the measured 180-nm device I-V and variability project 14,123 TOPS/W/bit and 412.2 TOPS/mm²/bit, respectively.

title: Contrastive Learning for Modeling Sensitive Attributes in Fairness-Aware Recommendation_supp1-3676999.pdf
authors: Shenghao Liu
abstract: Recently, the research on fairness in recommendation systems has garnered widespread attention. Moreover, numerous fair recommendation models have been developed for scenarios with limited sensitive information, thereby alleviating the issue of missing sensitive information. However, the performance of these methods still tends to decline significantly when sensitive attributes are extremely scarce. In this paper, we propose FairCL, a novel fair recommendation framework designed to perform effectively under limited sensitive attribute information. FairCL features a contrastive learning-based sensitive attribute encoder that can be integrated with existing fair recommendation algorithms. By leveraging both collaborative information and item side information, we predict unknown sensitive attributes and apply contrastive learning for sensitive attribute modeling. Furthermore, we theoretically demonstrate how FairCL can be integrated with mutual information-based and adversarial learning-based fairness algorithms. Extensive experiments on three real-world datasets show that FairCL significantly enhances fairness, even when only a small portion of users' sensitive attributes are known. The code and data are at: https://anonymous.4open.science/r/CL-for-FairRec-000A/.

title: Physical Layer Security of Coupled Phase Shifts STAR-RIS-Aided NOMA System under Hybrid Far- and Near-Field Scenarios
authors: Yuang Chen, Lei Shi, Zhiqing Tang, Lingfeng Shen, Wanming Hao, Jie Li
abstract: Near-field (NF) communications have attracted considerable interest, particularly with the implementation of extremely large-scale antenna arrays (ELAA). Additionally, the increase in communication frequencies and the expansion of reconfigurable intelligent surface (RIS) apertures contribute to this growing field. This paper investigates the synergy of simultaneously transmitting and reflecting (STAR)-RIS and non-orthogonal multiple access (NOMA) for secure transmission under hybrid far-field (FF) and NF scenarios. The secrecy sum rate (SSR) maximization problem is formulated by joint optimization of the power allocation, the beamforming at access point (AP), and the transmission/reflection coefficients (TRCs). Specifically, we consider the transmit power budget, unit-norm conditions, coupled phase shifts (CPS), quality of service requirements, and decoding order. To tackle this extremely challenging problem, we combine the successive convex approximation (SCA), Riemannian exact penalty method via smoothing, and penalty dual decomposition (PDD) and successfully develop an efficient iterative algorithm. Simulation results reveal that the proposed design exhibits superior effectiveness when compared to other traditional benchmarks.

title: Terminology Fragmentation of Infrastructure Calibration in Radio-Based Indoor Positioning: A Systematic Review_supp1-3680224.pdf
authors: Paul Schwarzbach

title: Learning-Based Robust Control of Electro-Ribbon Artificial Muscle
authors: Yuejun Xu, Majid Taghavi, Etienne Burdet
abstract: This article presents a learning-based robust control approach for electro-ribbon artificial muscle systems. The complex and coupled nonlinear electromechanical dynamics of these actuators make accurate modeling difficult. To address this challenge, we employ a neural network to approximate the actuator’s internal dynamics, with weight parameters set during an initial learning phase via gradient descent of a Lyapunov function. Feedback linearization combined with slidingmode control is then used to compensate for model estimation errors and achieve precise trajectory tracking, while an integral action mitigates steadystate uncertainties. A Lyapunov-function-based analysis establishes bounded estimation errors during the learning phase, and uniform ultimate boundedness of the closed-loop tracking errors. Experimental results under various operating conditions validate the novel control scheme, exhibiting improved tracking performance and robustness compared to conventional PID control.

title: 2026 Conference Calendar
authors: N/A

title: Spherical Stacked Intelligent Metasurfaces: A Paradigm for Full-Space Wave-Domain Processing
authors: Jiaqi Zhang, Qingchao Li, Kun Fang, Zhipeng Wang, Deyou Zhang, Hongyu Li, Bruno Clerckx
abstract: This article introduces the concept of spherical stacked intelligent metasurface (S-SIM), establishing a new paradigm for full-space electromagnetic field manipulation and wave-domain signal processing. By extending conventional planar stacked metasurfaces into a concentric spherical architecture, the S-SIM enables isotropic field interaction and full-space coverage. This article first establishes a unified modeling framework for S-SIM that integrates the architectural design, full-space wave-domain signal formulation, and channel representation to characterize inter-layer wave interactions and propagation characteristics. Building on this foundation, an iterative layer- by-layer beamforming algorithm is developed to optimize the phase responses of meta-atoms across multiple layers. Furthermore, a user-centric node division multiple access (NDMA) scheme is developed, allowing adaptive multiuser access and interference management in distributed three-dimensional environments. Case studies demonstrate that the proposed S-SIM system achieves more uniform full-space coverage and higher average spectral efficiency compared with the state-of-the-art (SoA) planar SIM. Finally, several open research directions are discussed, including hardware implementation, wave-domain computing, cooperative networking, and intelligent optimization, which lay the foundation on the practical deployment of S-SIM systems.

title: Toward Stable Semi-Supervised Remote Sensing Segmentation via Co-Guidance and Co-Fusion
authors: Yi Zhou, Xuechao Zou, Shun Zhang, Kai Li, Shiying Wang, Jingming Chen, Congyan Lang, Tengfei Cao, Pin Tao, Yuanchun Shi
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Semi-supervised remote sensing (RS) image semantic segmentation offers a promising solution to alleviate the burden of exhaustive annotation, yet it fundamentally struggles with pseudo-label drift, a phenomenon where confirmation bias leads to the accumulation of errors during training. In this work, we propose Co2S, a stable semi-supervised RS segmentation framework that synergistically fuses priors from vision-language models and self-supervised models. Specifically, we construct a heterogeneous dual-student architecture comprising two distinct ViT-based vision foundation models initialized with the weights of pre-trained CLIP and DINOv3 to mitigate error accumulation and pseudo-label drift. To effectively incorporate these distinct priors, a semantic co-guidance mechanism is introduced that utilizes text embeddings and learnable queries to provide explicit and implicit class-level guidance, respectively, thereby jointly enhancing semantic consistency. Furthermore, a global-local feature collaborative fusion strategy is developed to effectively fuse the global contextual information captured by CLIP with the local details produced by DINOv3, enabling the model to generate highly precise segmentation results. Extensive experiments on six popular datasets demonstrate the superiority of the proposed method, which consistently achieves leading performance across various partition protocols and diverse scenarios. Project page is available at https://xavierjiezou.github.io/Co2S/.

title: ChainVerFed: A Trustworthy Heterogeneous Federated Learning Framework with Chained Verification
authors: Jianbin Mai, Zhiquan Liu, Jingjing Guo, Yinbin Miao, Leping Li, Feixiang Ye
abstract: Federated learning (FL) for Consumer Electronics (CE) and consumer IoMT devices faces severe internal trust deficits. These are amplified in CE-centric IoMT deployments by model heterogeneity, data heterogeneity, and stringent privacy demands, manifesting as tamper-prone heterogeneous update histories, opaque and unauditable client aggregation, and inadequate resilience to malicious attacks. We propose ChainVerFed, a trustworthy FL framework for CE and consumer IoMT that incorporates chain-based verification via three innovations: Hierarchical Chain-aligned Latent Autoencoder (HCLAE), Hierarchical Dynamic Chain Clustering Attention (HDCCA), and Dual Exponential Chain Adaptive Privacy (DECAP). ChainVerFed delivers trustworthy, transparent, and secure collaboration across consumer IoMT devices common in CE scenarios. Experiments on consumer health monitoring datasets show consistent improvements over the compared methods, including up to 30.8% higher accuracy, 91.6% malicious-update filtering, and 2.9%–3.2% accuracy degradation under severe tampering. Theoretical analysis supports chain tamper detection, auditability, and attack robustness under the assumed threat model, underscoring Chain-VerFed’s potential for secure, verifiable learning in consumer electronics and consumer IoMT ecosystems.

title: A Mixed Reality System for Robust Manikin Localization in Childbirth Training
authors: Haojie Cheng, Chang Liu, Abhiram Kanneganti, Mahesh Arjandas Choolani, Arundhati Tushar Gosavi, Eng Tat Khoo
publish: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
abstract: Opportunities for medical students to gain practical experience in vaginal births are increasingly constrained by shortened clinical rotations, patient reluctance, and the unpredictable nature of labour. To alleviate clinicians' instructional burden and enhance trainees' learning efficiency, we introduce a mixed reality (MR) system for childbirth training that combines virtual guidance with tactile manikin interaction, thereby preserving authentic haptic feedback while enabling independent practice without continuous on-site expert supervision. The system extends the passthrough capability of commercial head-mounted displays (HMDs) by spatially calibrating an external RGB-D camera, allowing real-time visual integration of physical training objects. Building on this capability, we implement a coarse-to-fine localization pipeline that first aligns the maternal manikin with fiducial markers to define a delivery region and then registers the pre-scanned neonatal head within this area. This process enables spatially accurate overlay of virtual guiding hands near the manikin, allowing trainees to follow expert trajectories reinforced by haptic interaction. Experimental evaluations demonstrate that the system achieves accurate and stable manikin localization on a standalone headset, ensuring practical deployment without external computing resources. A large-scale user study involving 83 fourth-year medical students was subsequently conducted to compare MR-based and virtual reality (VR)-based childbirth training. Four senior obstetricians independently assessed performance using standardized criteria. Results showed that MR training achieved significantly higher scores in delivery, post-delivery, and overall task performance, and was consistently preferred by trainees over VR training. Although validated only in the context of obstetric delivery, the system demonstrates strong potential for broader manikin-based procedural training and other healthcare education scenarios.

title: Accessible Sound Design for Podcast Audio Engineering by Deaf and Hard of Hearing Creators
authors: Keita Ohshiro
abstract: None

title: Bridging Theory and Practice: Robust Deep Reinforcement Learning for IRS-Assisted IoT Under Imperfect CSI
authors: Fatima Tuz Zehra, Syed Asad Ullah, Ali Arshad Nasir, Muhammad Ali Jamshed, Syed Ali Hassan
abstract: Intelligent reflecting surfaces (IRS) coupled with deep reinforcement learning (DRL) promise significant gains for energy harvesting (EH) Internet-of-things (IoT) networks. However, most of the existing literature assumes perfect channel state information (CSI) while modeling, which is an assumption that is invalidated in real deployments with noisy and outdated information. This work introduces a realistic framework for training DRL agents under channel imperfections characterized by estimation errors, temporal correlation, and quantized feedback. We explore a case study of IRS-assisted EH in smart city environments, which demonstrates practical deployment considerations across diverse IoT applications. Comprehensive experiments were conducted using deep deterministic policy gradient (DDPG), twin delayed DDPG (TD3), proximal policy optimization (PPO), and soft actor-critic (SAC). Results demonstrate a 31% throughput improvement with the introduction of just 20 IRS elements as compared to a non-IRS baseline under perfect CSI when averaged across the four algorithms. Moreover, under moderately imperfect channel conditions, the best-performing algorithm in each CSI scenario exhibits no more than 10% degradation relative to the perfect CSI benchmark. These findings provide actionable deployment guidelines for IRS-assisted EH IoT systems operating under real-world channel conditions.

title: Decentralized topology robustness optimization for IoT via Multi-Agent Graph Reinforcement Learning
authors: Yabin Peng, Jiangxing Wu, Tong Duan, Yuchen Liu, Chenyu Zhou, Zhen Zhang
abstract: The robustness of Internet of Things (IoT) communication topologies against internal failures and external perturbations is a fundamental prerequisite for maintaining system stability. This paper studies IoT topology robustness from two perspectives: robustness metric and optimization. Existing robustness metrics, primarily based on the maximum connected subgraph, neglect contributions from other connected subgraphs, thereby inadequately capturing dynamic topological changes. To address this issue, we propose a robustness metric based on topology data reachability, which sensitively reflects the data transmission capability of an IoT topology under arbitrary perturbations. Regarding robustness optimization, most existing methods adopt centralized strategies that rely on global information, resulting in inefficiencies and limited adaptability in decentralized IoT environments. We propose DecTRO, a Decentralized Topology Robustness Optimization method for IoT via multi-agent graph reinforcement learning. To mitigate partial observability, DecTRO employs a scalable graph attention network enhanced with multi-modal sampling, which aggregates cross-agent information and captures spatiotemporal correlations. Furthermore, a topology robustness-oriented node sampling method is introduced to reduce action-space complexity and accelerate convergence, while a decentralized heuristic reward function enables efficient online decentralized learning. Experimental results show that DecTRO achieves up to two orders of magnitude (5–125×) greater improvement in robustness per unit time compared with state-of-the-art baselines, striking a favorable balance between robustness enhancement and computational efficiency.

title: Mechanism Analysis of AC Breakdown Voltage Reduction in Short Air Gaps under Combined Strong Wind and Heavy Rain
authors: Lin Yang, Jiabao Ding, Peng Fu, Xingkun Hu, Quan Liu, Licheng Li, Junhan Lu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dielectrics and Electrical Insulation
abstract: Strong winds and rainstorms can easily cause windage yaw discharge tripping accidents on transmission lines, which seriously affects the safe operation of power grids. At present, the AC breakdown characteristics of short air gaps under different rainfall intensities and wind speeds have rarely been reported. In this paper, the 10-40 cm rod-rod and rod-plate short air gap AC discharge tests were carried out under combined wind and rain conditions. The test results show that the air gap breakdown voltage is further reduced under combined wind and rain conditions compared to windless condition. Considering the most extreme conditions of rainfall intensity I=15 mm/min and wind speed v=24 m/s, the maximum reduction of the rod-rod and rod-plate gap breakdown voltages are 30.8% and 26.0%, respectively, comparing with the windless and rainless conditions. The reduction of the breakdown voltage of rod-rod and rod-plate gaps is reduced gradually with the increase of the gap distance. It is analyzed that the water flow at the end of the high-voltage rod electrode shortens the gap insulation distance and intensifies the distortion of the electric field caused by the raindrops under the combined wind and rain conditions. Meanwhile, the influence of wind makes the motion path of the ions in the gap closer to the axis of the gap, which strengthens the electric field intensity of the gap and makes the discharge more likely to occur.

title: Mismatched Pairs Dynamic Correction for Cross-Modal Alignment in Video Moment Retrieval
authors: Hongxiao Hu, Ran Ran, Jiwei Wei, Chengwei Sun, Yuyang Zhou, Shiyuan He, Kuien Liu, Yang Yang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
abstract: Video Moment Retrieval (VMR) aims to locate and identify specific moments in a video based on a natural language query. However, most existing methods overlook the issue of mismatched pairs (i.e., false negatives), wherein semantically similar or nearly identical queries and videos are erroneously treated as negative instances. To address this problem, we propose Mismatched Pairs Dynamic Correction (MPDC), a novel approach that rectifies such mismatched pairs on-the-fly without requiring additional annotations or intricate procedures. Specifically, we first construct both intra- and inter-modal similarity matrices for text-text, video-video, and text-video pairs, then aggregate these matrices to effectively detect previously overlooked mismatched pairs. We subsequently address these mismatches by adaptively updating their matching relationships during training, ensuring a dynamic and accurate correction process. Furthermore, to enhance the distinguishability of multi-modal features, we incorporate auxiliary queries derived from these similarity matrices to generate discriminative saliency scores, which serve as weights to refine the original feature representations. Our method is plug-and-play, seamlessly integrating into existing architectures and significantly boosting their performance. Extensive experiments conducted on four benchmark datasets (QVHighlights, Charades-STA, ActivityNet-Captions, and TACoS) demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance.

title: High Performance Compact Microstrip Leaky-Wave Antenna Based on Curved Meander-line with Flexible Dispersive Behavior
authors: Mohammad Hafiez, Jayanta Ghosh
publish: IEEE Antennas and Wireless Propagation Letters
abstract: A curved meander-line based microstrip leaky-wave antenna (CMLWA) is presented here. The introduction of the curved section makes the antenna compact and efficient, with a net electrical length of 4.71λ0. A novel technique i.e, the width modulation of the curved section, is utilized to achieve impedance matching and to suppress the open stop-band. Additionally, it provides a flexible dispersive behavior for both K and Ku bands. Harmonic analysis of the proposed design is done using Brillouin diagram. The full-length CMLWA is very efficient and compact. Over the entire operating band, efficiency exceeds 75%, with a maximum of 97%. It provides a wide, symmetric scan of 106° (-48° to 58°) across the operating frequency range of 12.3 15.3 GHz. The gain variation is 3.3 dBi with a peak gain of 14.1 dBi. A prototype of the proposed configuration has been fabricated and measured. The results of the proposed antenna provide considerable advantages over the leaky-wave antenna reported in past literature.

title: Neural Differential Equations with Integrated Control for Chemical Process Modeling
authors: Sumeet Rodiya, Ved Vartak, Kunal Bahuguna, Subhajit Sanfui, Ramsatish Kaluri
publish: 2025 IEEE 32nd International Conference on High Performance Computing, Data and Analytics Workshop (HiPCW)
abstract: Neural differential equations have emerged as powerful tools for chemical process systems simulations, but their implementation with controlled chemical processes is relatively unexplored. This paper presents a study of neural ordinary differential equations (NODEs) and neural controlled differential equations (NCDEs) for chemical process modeling with explicit control integration for a batched bioreactor system. We develop a modified NODE framework incorporating the control inputs. We also study the NCDE framework which inherently adds control variables to their continuous path allowing the network to learn control dependency via spline interpolation. Both frameworks are implemented using Diffrax, enabling quick and efficient GPU-accelerated training. Evaluation on batched bioreactor problem shows that NCDEs achieve superior accuracy while NODEs provide a faster training.

title: Performance Characterization of Data Transfer and Allocation Strategies on AMD MI300A APUs: Early Experiences
authors: Goutham Kalikrishna Reddy Kuncham, Siyuan Zhang, Bharath Ramesh, Kaushik Kandadi Suresh, Dhabaleswar K. Panda
publish: 2025 IEEE 32nd International Conference on High Performance Computing, Data and Analytics Workshop (HiPCW)
abstract: AMD MI300A APU, the first data center-class APU with a unified HBM3 memory pool for both CPU and GPU, offers the potential to bypass traditional data copy bottlenecks. While this design simplifies memory sharing, it introduces challenges in determining optimal memory allocation and data transfer strategies. In this paper, we present a comprehensive performance characterization of memory allocation (Malloc vs. hipMalloc) and data transfer methods (CPU-based memcpy, GPU-driven hipMemcpy, and kernel-based copies) on the MI300A. We systematically report bandwidth and latency for intra-APU, inter-APU, and inter-node scenarios, offering actionable insights to help developers fully leverage APUs for HPC and AI workloads.

title: A Comprehensive Evaluation of Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning on Code Smell Detection
authors: Beiqi Zhang, Peng Liang, Xin Zhou, Xiyu Zhou, David Lo, Qiong Feng, Zengyang Li, Lin Li
publish: ACM Transactions on Software Engineering and Methodology
abstract: None

title: SincQDR-VAD: A Noise-Robust Voice Activity Detection Framework Leveraging Learnable Filters and Ranking-Aware Optimization
authors: Chien-Chun Wang, En-Lun Yu, Jeih-Weih Hung, Shih-Chieh Huang, Berlin Chen
publish: 2025 IEEE Automatic Speech Recognition and Understanding Workshop (ASRU)
abstract: Voice activity detection (VAD) is essential for speech-driven applications, but remains far from perfect in noisy and resource-limited environments. Existing methods often lack robustness to noise, and their frame-wise classification losses are only loosely coupled with the evaluation metric of VAD. To address these challenges, we propose SincQDR-VAD, a compact and robust framework that combines a Sinc-extractor front-end with a novel quadratic disparity ranking loss. The Sinc-extractor uses learnable bandpass filters to capture noise-resistant spectral features, while the ranking loss optimizes the pairwise score order between speech and non-speech frames to improve the area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUROC). A series of experiments conducted on representative benchmark datasets show that our framework considerably improves both AUROC and $F_{2}$-Score, while using only $69 \%$ of the parameters compared to prior arts, confirming its efficiency and practical viability.

title: IEEE VR 2026 Organizing Committee
authors: N/A
